content.full_text:
MEYERS, NAVE, RIBACK & WEST

MICHAEL J NAVE
STEVEN N MEYERS
NATALIE E WEST
ELIZABETH M SILVER
MICHAEL S RIBACK
MOLLY P TAMI
MICHAEL P RODRIGUEZ
KATHLEEN FAUBION
FREDERICK S ETHERIDGE
WENDY A ROBERTS
DAVID W SKINNER

A PROFESSIONAL LAW CORPORATION

GATEWAY PLAZA
777 DAVIS STREET, SUITE 300
SAN LEANDRO, CALIFORNIA 94577

TELEPHONE (510) 351-4300
FACSIMILE (510) 351-4481

PENINSULA OFFICE

1220 HOWARD AVE., SUITE 280
BURLINGAME, CA 94010-1711

TELEPHONE (415) 344-7130
FACSIMILE (415) 344-0588

MARIN OFFICE

1202 GRANT AVE., SUITE E
NOVATO, CA 94945

TELEPHONE (415) 892-8874

OF COUNSEL
ANDREA J. SALTZMAN

REPLY TO:

San Leandro

December 13, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
Acting General Counsel
428 J. Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Request for Advice Letter on Designated Employees

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This firm represents the Fire Districts Association of California - Fire Association Self Insurance System (hereinafter "FDAC"), a joint powers authority comprised of fire districts. We are requesting an advice letter concerning the filing of economic interest statements under the Political Reform Act.

The FDAC is a joint powers authority created pursuant to the California Government Code. The FDAC manages its members' workers compensation matters and currently has 95 member districts from throughout both Northern and Southern California. Each member district elects one representative to the FDAC Board of Directors.

Because of the large size of the 95-member Board and the fact that its member districts are located throughout the state, holding frequent meetings and operating the FDAC by the full 95-member Board would be extremely cumbersome. Therefore, the FDAC has developed the following governing system.

The full 95-member Board meets only once a year to elect directors to a seven-member Executive Committee. At its annual meeting, the full Board also appoints Executive Committee officers, adopts the FDAC budget, votes on admitting new member districts, and adopts an annual audit. The Board is not involved in the day-to-day operation of the FDAC. (In fact, a typical Board meeting is completed in less than an hour.)

The seven-member Executive Committee meets three to four times a year and is responsible for the operation of the FDAC. For example, the Executive Committee makes decisions on contracts, investments, claim processing and the work of consultants. For all intensive purposes, it is the Executive Committee which serves as

Scott Hallabrin
December 13, 1991
Page 2


the "Board" of FDAC, with the 95-member Board meeting briefly once
a year to appoint the Executive Committee and its officers.

     The FDAC is in the process of adopting its conflict of
interest code pursuant to the Political Reform Act.  In drafting
the necessary documents, we, as counsel for FDAC, request your
advice on the following question concerning "designated employees":
while it seems clear that the position of director of the Executive
Committee must be a designated position because the directors make
decisions which may foreseeably have an effect on a financial
interest, must the position of Board member on the 95-member Board
be listed as a designated position?

     Given the rather limited activities of the Board, which
consist primarily of electing the Executive Committee, and the fact
that listing the position of Board member as a designated employee
would result in 95 persons filing economic interest statements, we
believe that Board members should not be subject to this require-
ment of filing statements of economic interest.

     If you have any questions or need further information, please
do not hesitate to contact us.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        MEYERS, NAVE, RIBACK & WEST

                                        [signature]
                                        Fred S. Etheridge

309\ltr\hallabrin.fse

bcc: Darrell Heppner
